data.title: CrowdStrike: Nothing, Even Growth, Can Last Forever
data.description:
CrowdStrike Holdings (NASDAQ:CRWD) has soared alongside the rest of the tech sector as its software business proved resilient to tariff headwinds. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet and solid profit margins. The market looks overly focused on the near-term possibility of accelerating topline growth, which is a curious point given that the aggressive valuation would instead require a long-term view. I fail to make the math work here for market-beating returns. I reiterate my "Neutral" rating for the stock.

CRWD Stock Price

I last covered CRWD in April, where I recommended avoiding the stock due to valuation. The stock has since roared 27% higher.

The momentum is strong here, but I think the market is underestimating the potential downward pressure as growth inevitably slows down.

CRWD Stock Key Metrics

CRWD is a giant in the cybersecurity industry, first known for its endpoint protections but now known for having one of the most complete cybersecurity portfolios.

In the most recent quarter, CRWD generated 19.8% YoY revenue growth to $1.103 billion, coming in at the middle of guidance of between $1.1006 billion and $1.1064 billion. In spite of the macro environment, investors were likely hoping for CRWD to beat guidance as it typically does.

The company generated $201 million in non-GAAP operating income, crushing guidance of between $173.1 million and $180 million. While it is just one quarter, I should note that it is common for high-growth companies to eventually prioritize the bottom line (including guidance beats) as they mature and growth slows down.

CRWD ended the quarter with $4.6 billion of cash versus $744 million of debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet. The company authorized a $1 billion share repurchase program, which is an interesting yet not quite unprecedented decision as other richly valued names like Snowflake (SNOW) have also been repurchasing shares.

Looking ahead, management guided for the company to see 19.5% YoY revenue growth in the second quarter and left the full-year guidance intact, which calls for 21.5% YoY growth.

On the conference call, management discussed their layoff of around 5% of their workforce in May, noting that they expect this to "add at least 1% to next year's non-GAAP operating margin from our previously discussed target," culminating in "at least 24%" margins in fiscal 2027 and a free cash flow margin of "more than 30%." Management expects adoption of new products to drive the implied acceleration in revenue growth in the second half of the year. We have seen Zscaler (ZS) successfully transition into a projected acceleration after its own first-half slowdown, so perhaps CRWD can do the same.

Is CRWD Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

As of recent prices, CRWD traded at around 24x sales. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain 20% topline growth for many years.

That outlook looks too aggressive, in my view. The company has sustained aggressive growth rates in large part due to its wide product portfolio, which has allowed it to cross sell to existing customers. This is not a sustainable strategy long term as it works through its customer base.

Sure, one could make an argument that CRWD's products are top tier and mission critical, making it "inevitable" for successful cross selling. But again. It isn't clear just how much more juice is left in the tank. I expect that uncertainty to eventually proliferate in the stock price. Even if we assume that the company can meet aggressive consensus estimates, then the stock doesn't look cheap here. I can see the stock trading at around 10x sales in 2034, which might prove too aggressive given the likely drop off in growth rates. Based on 35% long-term net margins, that would equate to a 29x earnings multiple. The stock is priced for around 7.5% annual returns through 2034 assuming it hits consensus estimates and trades at a premium 10x sales multiple by then as well. Given the aggressive underlying assumptions, I view that return proposition as being woefully insufficient. I would be more interested in the name if the stock traded down to the 12x to 14x sales multiple range. While that would still represent a premium to the 6x to 8x multiples that I'm seeing in my coverage, this premium would be warranted given the more aggressive growth rates.

CRWD Stock Conclusion

CRWD, like many software names, has shown great resilience despite macropressures. Management has also implied an acceleration in topline growth exiting the year due to new product adoption. However, I highlight the underappreciated risk that the cross-selling strategy underpinning recent resilient growth rates will eventually fade as it saturates its customer base. I recommend investors search for cheaper tech names, as I have done for the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Neutral" rating for the stock.

data.title: Service Corporation Q2: As Steady As She Goes
data.description:
I assigned a Strong Buy rating to Service Corporation (NYSE:SCI) in April 2025, discussing its structural growth from aging populations. The company delivered a strong quarter with solid confidence in the growth in the second half of FY25. I reiterate a Strong Buy rating with a fair value of $101 per share.

Service Corporation released its Q2 FY25 on July 30, posting a 3.1% growth in comparable total funeral sales growth and 5.3% growth in cemetery preneed sales productions. As detailed in the table below, the company achieved 11% adj. EPS growth, a strong margin improvement during the quarter.

My key takeaway from the quarter was the management’s confidence in the second half of FY25. During the call, the management anticipated both preneed cemetery sales production and preneed funeral sales production will grow by low-to-mid single digit in the second half of FY25. Because of the strong performance, the company targets to achieve $880 million to $940 million in operating cash flow for FY25, with around $600 million in adjusted FCF. On an adjusted basis, the current stock price is trading at less than 18x free cash flow, a low level in my view.

Another highlight is the company’s share repurchase and generous capital return policy. YTD, the company has already repurchased $320 million in own shares, which is quite significant considering its total market capitalization of $10.7 billion. The company exited the quarter with a 3.68x net debt leverage, within its target range of 3.5 to 4x. As such, I think its strong balance sheet could support the company’s ongoing share repurchase activities. The total number of shares outstanding declined by 4.5% in FY23, followed by another 3.5% decrease in FY24. I estimate that the ongoing shares buyback will at least reduce its total number of shares by 3% annually in the near future.

As detailed in the slide below, Service Corporation is guiding for $3.85 in adj. EPS at the midpoint for FY25.

As discussed in my previous article, Service Corporation operates a very stable business model. I anticipate the company will deliver 3% revenue growth in FY25, considering its direct sales transition from trust to insurance model. As noted during the call, the company has already transitioned 95% of total productions, and anticipates the direct sales will return to growth in early FY26. As such, from FY26 onwards, I forecast the company will deliver 5% organic revenue growth. In addition, I anticipate the company will allocate 3% of revenue towards acquisitions in the funeral service market, contributing to an additional 1.3% to the topline growth.

I forecast the company will deliver 30bps annual margin expansion, driven by 10bps from pricing, and 20bps from operating leverage of SG&A. I calculate the normalized operating costs will grow by 5.8% annually, leading to an operating margin of 25.7% by FY34.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate FCFF as below:

I revised WACC to be 8.7% assuming: risk free rate 4.2%; beta 0.9; equity risk premium 7%; cost of debt 5%; tax rate 25%; equity $11 billion; debt $4.8 billion. I set the terminal growth rate to be 4%, aligning with the overall market growth. With these assumptions, I calculate the total enterprise value to be $18.7 billion in total and the fair value to be $101 per share after adjusting net cash and debt positions.

Key Risk

In the U.S. market, many Hispanic and Asian customers prefer cemetery services. Due to the Trump administration’s immigration policy, I think some Hispanic and Asian populations might leave the U.S. If that occurs, the population mix change could pose some growth challenges to Service Corporation.

In addition, the gross margin of its cemetery business declined by 110bps during the quarter, caused by higher sales compensations. Investors need to closely monitor the company’s gross margin in upcoming quarters to ensure these elevated sales compensation payments do not become a persistent issue.

Conclusion

Service Corporation delivered very solid second quarter results, and the management remains confident in its core production growth for the second half of FY25. I think its stock price is undervalued and reiterate a Strong Buy rating with a fair value of $101 per share.

data.title: Axos Financial: Solid Growth, But Wait To Pull The Trigger
data.description:
Today we check in on and cover the just-reported earnings Axos Financial (NYSE:AX) One thing to note unlike many other banking institutions we cover, there is no dividend here. Instead the company reinvests for growth. Thus far here in this quarter, our coverage of financials and regional banks, which we consider 'pulse-checks' on local economies, indicates the economy has been resilient as a whole. There have been little to no signs of fiscal stress despite risks from tariffs, and somewhat softening economic data. While performance varies depending on the bank, the sector has been strong. Overall, the key metrics have held up very well. is the holding company for Axos Bank, Axos Clearing LLC and Axos Invest, Inc. Axos Bank is provides consumer and business banking products nationwide, and also provides comprehensive securities clearing services to introducing broker-dealers and registered investment advisor correspondents, as well as digital investment advisory services to retail investors. So, it is a bit more specialized than the average consumer focused bank, but is a strong performer. Let us discuss.

Axos Bank headline results and net interest income

Axos saw revenue of $321.5 million, which was a 10.5% increase in this critical metric year-over-year, and beat estimates by $9.2 million. What about margins? We saw strength here as well. Net interest margin rose from the sequential Q3 by 6 basis points to 4.84%, which is incredibly strong, and above average in the banking sector based on our universe of coverage. Net interest income and non-interest income increased by $4.7 million and $7.9 million, respectively, on a linked quarter basis, to $276.7 million and $256.5 million, respectively. This is strong.

We should also note that the provision for credit losses was $15 million, mostly reflecting the new assets held and a reserve built out. We monitor this metric from this current level as provisions do offset earnings power, and the provisions were up from $6 million a year ago. For the fiscal year provisions were $55.8 million up from $32.5 million. EPS (adjusted) for the quarter was a very strong $1.94, reflecting growth in loans, and strong margins. These were up from $1.83 in the comparable year ago quarter, and for the fiscal year, EPS was $7.50, up from $6.74.

Axos Bank's loans, deposits, and asset quality

We always monitor trends in loans, deposits, and key asset quality metrics. Loans were up $855 million sequentially. Loans, net of credit loss allowances, finished the quarter at $21.05 billion, a huge increase from $19.23 billion a year ago. It was a tremendous fiscal year.

Given the huge increase in the loan book, the allowance for credit losses rose from last year. These allowances were up to $290.05 million, which was up from $260.54 to start the year.

A more meaningful metric is the allowance for credit losses as a percentage of all loans: this was 1.36% compared to 1.34% to start the year. This is a bump, which suggests additional allowances are being made on both the back of higher loans, and perhaps some risk of non-payment associated with select loans. Net charge-offs were 0.16% of average loans, up from 0.05% a year ago. Thus, we do see some softening of asset quality, and this is something that needs to be monitored.

We can also still look at the return metrics to understand the earnings power here. We saw more loans and higher margins which is strong. The return on average assets was up 4 basis points from the year ago quarter to 1.85%. However for the fiscal year, returns on average assets were down 26 basis points to 1.82%, from 2.08%. This is a negative. We saw a weaker return on equity of 16.85% in the quarter, down from 18.81% a year ago, while for the fiscal year, this metrics was down 434 basis points to 17.30%. For the fiscal year we also saw less efficiency. That said, the efficiency of the bank is better than the average bank we cover, hitting 46.84%, compared to 43.59% in the prior fiscal year.

Stock is expensive relative to book value, but book value is expanding

We love to buy bank stocks when the key metrics are sound when share prices are close to book or even tangible book. In today's market, this is a rare find. Axos trades at a significant premium, but you are paying for the growth. Shares are at $88 at the time of this writing, and that is a huge premium-to-book value, which was $47.46 to end the quarter. However, this is up from $40.26 to start the fiscal year. Tangible book was up to $44.60 from $37.26 a year ago. Still, this is a significant premium.

Final thoughts

This is a growing bank, with some mixed key metric performance. We are not concerned with the softening of asset quality metrics, though these need to be monitored. But we think it is best to wait for a broader market pull back before entering shares, because there is a significant premium relative to book value. While book value, and as such the true value of the bank is growing, following loans, revenues, and earnings power, higher, we think it is best to wait.

data.title: AMD Earnings Preview: No Exit Sign At Q2
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) is set to report its Q2 '25 earnings call in early August on the 5th, and the stock is up around 126% since April lows, getting another bump of 25% over the past month alone, outperforming Nvidia (NVDA) on both fronts, with the latter up 86% and 11%, respectively. AMD is also pretty expensive, even relative to NVDA, with a forward P/E of 78x compared to NVDA’s at 42x. So, safe to say, AMD is turning into new money’s next favorite AI pick for the semis for the year, and that’ll keep pushing the stock higher for the short-term, in our opinion. Come Q3 '25, we’ll be having a different conversation.

Heading into next quarter’s print, there’s a big question mark around whether gains will hold, particularly considering that AMD has a track record of trading down post-earnings reports. This, coupled with the market’s overwhelmingly bullish sentiment on AMD intra-quarter, put the stock at higher risk heading into print. Remember that AMD stock saw a bump up on the U.S. pause on H20/MI308 ban to China and the potential raised pricing of MI350 AI chips, all of which happened this month.

While these positives are priced into the stock, excitement is still building around AMD’s AI story. Our thesis is that the reason to buy AMD is not AI. Instead, we upgraded the stock on the May 6th report because that's when we felt the AI story got de-risked, and upside from their CPU share gain would support growth. The stock is up around 78% since our upgrade, as shown below. The AMD hype story is building up again, and we see enough fuel in the tank to ride the wave in the short term. We now have a Buy call on the stock.

What are the moving parts heading into Q3 print?

This print could go different ways, mainly cause of the stock intra-quarter run up, but overall, we think the trajectory in 2H25 will be up for AMD. There are three moving parts to keep tabs on this quarter:

#1 Higher Average Selling Price for MI350

AMD is apparently thinking about raising prices for its Instinct MI350 AI accelerator to $25,000 from $15,000, which would be an almost 70% higher average selling price. The market is taking this as a sign that AMD is seeing accelerating demand for its AI products. The alternative could be that AMD is slowly adjusting its whole spiel about using attractive pricing to lure in customers. It could also be related to margin expansion since even with that potential price hike, however, AMD’s products would be cheaper than their NVDA equivalent, B200, priced $30,000-$40,000. AMD's gross margins contracted 40 basis points last quarter due to lower sales. Regardless, it's a sign of confidence for AMD's AI business.

For MI355X, which AMD is now ramping, the price is expected to be in the $30,000 neighborhood. There hasn’t been any comment from management on this, as far as we know. Nonetheless, we think this is a sign that excitement around the AI story is building up again. HSBC noted that the:

MI350 can now compete with NVDA's Blackwell B200 in the market...AMD's AI chip sales next year will be significantly higher than the prior estimate of $9.6B, at $15.1B.

The stock was up over 4% on Monday on the report, and another 2% on Tuesday. Nonetheless, if we get confirmation of a price hike it would be an adjustment to AMD's price-to-performance attraction.

This is a double-edged sword; it’s good for us in the short term because we think it’ll lead to investors taking every piece of news as a cup-half-full story.

#2 Share gain against Intel sprinkled with tariff pull-in

The other factor to take into account here is tariff pull-in because, despite a lot of denial we saw last quarter, tariff pull-in is in the air this quarter, and even AMD can’t escape it. Intel (INTC) management embraced the higher sequential growth as a result of tariff pull-in, and we’re likely to see a similar impact on AMD’s report. What should help offset this in the case of AMD is continued share gain against INTC in the CPU market, both on the Client and Server fronts. AMD now captures “around a third of every dollar spent on server CPUs,” while INTC slipped from almost full dominance to a 63% share. Expectations from IDC and Mercury Research estimated AMD to take home a 36% share of the server CPU market this year. Other reports place that number higher, surpassing 40% share of the market in AMD’s favor and hitting 60% in 2026.

This is all positive for AMD, but it’s also not going to be an effortless fight for share, particularly with Arm (ARM) estimated to be a strong contender. Arm’s CPU share against x86 crossed into double-digit territory, up sequentially to 11.9% according to Mercury Research report. The same report added that there’s also a surge in demand for AMD’s Client CPUs, which is pushing this share gain. The reality is that everyone is getting brownie points, except Intel. So, the combination of share gain momentum and tariff pull in should result in better Client sales for AMD, in our opinion.

#3 Renewed License application to ship MI308 AI Chips to China

Back in April, AMD and NVDA got kicked out of selling their AI GPUs in China, including the MI308 and H20, respectively. This caused massive write-offs, including $800M write-off for AMD. Earlier this month, the U.S. paused that ban, which caused AMD to trade up around 6%, higher than NVDA did on that same piece of news. Now the entire market is expecting $800M in MI308 inventory to be sold at “near-zero cost with high gross margins.” We think the market could be getting a little ahead of itself when it comes to what’ll happen to that inventory going forward. Keep in mind that Jean X. Hu, AMD CFO, noted on the Bank of America conference call the following about Q2:

When we look at Q2, that's Vivek, what you're seeing is there are a lot of noises and a lot of uncertainties. And one of the things that really impact AMD is the export license requirement for MI308. When you think about it is, we were expecting the first half data center revenue to be flattish with the second half of 2024 as we were going through the product transition from MI325 to MI350 but because of the export license requirement, we got impacted by $700 million revenue in Q2 alone with the data center GPU business. So it was quite significant. Of course, in China, because of the DeepSeek surge of the demand for GPUs during that period of time.

So the market is going to be expecting a comeback after that headwind has been eliminated. Still, we think this will only build AMD’s AI story, not put it to the test in the short term. It’s on the savvy investor to take it with a pinch of salt. We do know, however, that AMD hasn’t been doing badly in attracting attention of its own; the company showed off the OpenAI CEO at their AMD AI Day in June, during which they also noted that Instinct chips are being adopted by seven of the ten big AI players, including Tesla. Oracle is also on that list, with AMD developing a cluster of 30,000 AMD MI355X GPUs in a multi-billion-dollar contract. On the Q1 call, Lisa Su noted that :

We also added multiple tier one cloud and enterprise customers in the quarter, including one of the largest frontier model developers that is now using Instinct GPUs to serve a significant portion of their daily inference traffic.

AMD’s chips are also doing pretty well for themselves. In terms of memory bandwidth, the MI355X offers 22.1 TB/s, nearly three times the 8 TB/s of the B200. When it comes to compute power, FP16 performance of 3,000–4,000 TFLOPS (dense) is closing the gap with Nvidia's 5,000 TFLOPS (sparse), narrowing the performance disparity. So, although we believe NVDA will continue to be the industry's go-to, we think AMD is doing okay for itself.

Q2 Preview: What to do with the stock?

AMD guided for flattish sequential growth last quarter for revenue of $7.40B versus estimates for $7.24B. The market is now expecting closer to $7.41B for 27% Y/Y growth.

Here’s what management guided for when it comes to the nitty gritty:

  • Client sales are expected to grow double-digit sequentially, which is better than typical seasonality.

  • Data center sales, on the other hand, are expected to decline because of the U.S. export restrictions on China.

  • Non-GAAP gross margin is expected to contract to 43% due to inventory write-down.

This is how AMD performed last quarter:

We see upside for AMD on its CPU sales, more so than its AI GPU ones. Never forget what AMD’s bread and butter is at the end of the day: Client and Server CPUs. The chart below outlines AMD’s results last quarter. We don’t get a clear-cut AI GPU sales number for the quarter, but we think a lot of the upside is from the CPU side for AMD, including share against INTC. We agree with Lisa Su when she said at the Bank of America conference:

On the CPU side, we actually have the best product portfolio with both our server CPU and the client CPU.

We remain buy-rated on AMD heading into next print and see room for this growth to stretch out a bit longer.

data.title: Inside Meta's Q2 Earnings: Ad System Upgrades And WhatsApp Fuel Growth
data.description:
Meta (NASDAQ:META) delivered incredible Q2 results despite some pessimism surrounding the company leading up to the report.

Tariffs mostly weighed down on sentiment, and price actions leading up to the results reflected these concerns.

However, as we had discussed in our last analysis, we expected results to be on the higher end of the forecast provided by management, with a large potential for a beat. Our reasoning was due to the timing of the guidance, which was shortly after the tariff announcements from President Trump in April.

While the fickleness of advertisers and their willingness to spend may be an issue, well-positioned companies typically continue to spend money on effective advertisement channels.

Meta's Q2 2025 Results

Meta delivered Q2 revenue of $47.52B, which was a 22% YoY increase compared to the forecasted 9-16% jump ($42.5-45.5B).

CAPEX guidance continued to tighten as we enter into Q3, with management forecasting $66-72B vs. previous estimates of $64-72B.

ARPP was $13.65 across Meta's 3.45B users, which in and of itself grew by another 50mm since Q1.

Revenue forecast for Q3 was $47.5-50.5B, marking a 17-24% YoY increase vs. the 19% achieved in 2024. This consistent growth of a company that is generating nearly $200B in annual revenue is impressive to say the least.

Management did, however, mention that YoY growth rate will slow as a result of the company lapping its phase of strong growth.

Consumer monetization efforts are underway across Thread and WhatsApp. While results are still early, the continued increase in usage is a positive signal, partly driven by Meta AI offered on WhatsApp.

Per the Q2 2025 earnings call:

WhatsApp continues to be the largest driver of queries as people message Meta AI directly for tasks such as information gathering, homework assistance, and generating images.

Similar to Q1, WhatsApp continues to drive a significant portion of Other Revenue as well, which we see as a positive signal that is still underappreciated by the market:

Family of apps other revenue was $583 million, up 50%, driven by whatsapp paid messaging revenue growth as well as meta verified subscriptions.

In our previous analysis, we pointed out three key factors that drive price action.

  • CAPEX commitments and forecasts
  • Revenue forecasts
  • Growth trajectories of Meta's Family of Apps, such as Reels, Threads, and WhatsApp

All three had a positive undertone, which contributed to the ~12% pop after-hours.

A Glimpse into Meta's Ad System

During Meta's earnings calls, we sometimes get a glimpse into Mark Zuckerberg's thinking on a particular strategy or clarity on the financial performance of a business unit whose numbers aren't broken out.

Mark Zuckerberg, of course, addressed the initiative of his SuperIntelligence Lab and the potential of AI to provide more utility to users, allowing them to spend time on activities they truly enjoy.

But what we found the most interesting was the additional color that he provided on their advertisement system and their modular approach. This revealed Meta's strategy of dissecting and individually optimizing the distinct components of their advertisement system, ensuring at the same time, systematic improvements that are all aimed at ultimately increasing conversion rates.

Meta's advertisement system is broadly separated into three buckets:

  • Adrometa
  • Generative Ads Recommendation System (GEM)
  • Lattice

The Andrometa system focuses on improving the ad retrieval stage so that the right ad candidates are on standby to be shown to the user. This narrows down the pool from tens of millions of ads to a few thousand of the most relevant ads. Interestingly, what seems to a fairly minor component of an advertisement system has shown to drive conversion 4% higher on both Facebook Mobile Feed and Reels. It's also where Meta's AI inference chip is being used, also known as Meta Training and Inference Accelerator.

The Generative Ads Recommendation System (GEM) determines which ads will be shown after the ads are narrowed down. This is the personalization engine that's been trained on thousands of GPUs. Some of the improvements included new data on engagement and longer history of the content or ads that a user had engaged with in the past. These improvements drove 5% higher conversion on Instagram and 3% higher conversion on both Feed and Reels.

Finally, Lattice drove another 4% bump in conversion on Feed and Reels, which takes a broader view of the advertisement ecosystem and allows generalization of learnings across campaign objectives and surfaces.

Collectively, this system has led to 5% more ad conversions on Instagram and 3% more ad conversions on Facebook, helping fuel the increasing advertisement dollar spent as people spend 5% and 6% more time on Facebook and Instagram, respectively. This has resulted in the average price per ad increasing by 9% for the quarter.

Closing Thoughts

Meta's Q2 2025 earnings gave a glimpse into what Meta is best at: advertising. It also showcased the second-order impact that Meta's founder-driven initiatives are having across its business segments, with the H100s bought during Meta's catch-up phase to Reels driving 1) more engagement, 2) improved recommendations, and 3) better positioning in AI. We continue to feel optimistic about the company's trajectory going into Q3.
